feedback.text: Foremost, Digital Fairness Act (DFA) is a type of regulation we desperately need, so thank you for putting this proposal forward. I would like to suggest adding another category to the act - which is customer protections in the field of videogames. Currently hundreds of games are regularly being remotely disabled and rendered unusable by their publishers, rendering products "dead". These are titles that were not sold with any mention of expiry date or subscription requirement. Due to the lack of clear regulation, the publishers feel at liberty to sell users a product, and then remotely disable it, based on previously undisclosed business reasons. Imagine buying a book that one day refuses to open, or a vinyl that suddenly won't play anymore. Unthinkable - but it is happening with a frightening frequency in the field of games. Over a Million EU citizens have signed the "Stop Destroying Videogames" petition on the European Citizens' Initiative - Online Collection System - that deals directly with the problem. Having the subject included in a larger act protecting customers of digital goods could help this practice come to a stop. Thank you.
